---
title: 'Data Types and Structures '
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: solarized
    resources: ["images"]
editor: visual
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: true
  message: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo =TRUE)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(error=TRUE)

library(tidyverse)

```

## <span style="color:orange"> Data types {.scrollable}

All programming languages use data-types, or structures to hold information

- integer
- floating point/ real / numeric
- character
- string

Often data types are multi-dimensional

Some useful ones in R

- vector
- matrix
- data frame
- tibble
- factors
- lists

Programming often involves selecting and building data structures

Good data structures are

- as simple as possible
- easy to understand (readable names)
- easy to manipulate
- easy to visualize

## Ways to generate data {.scrollable}

For testing your code, often helpful to generate *test* data

We also often generate random data to explore the behavior of our code and to understand how it works

- *runif(min,max)* (sampling from a uniform distribution)
- *rnorm(mean,sd)* (sampling from uniform and normal distributions)
- *rpois(lambda)* (sampling from a Poisson distribution)

(many other options for sampling from distributions)

## Other ways to generate data

- *sample(vector,size,replace)* (sampling from a vector of values)
- *seq(start,end,by)* (for creating a sequence of numbers)

## Examples {.scrollable}

- Fire starts - uniform distribution
- Otter weights - normal distribution
- Randomly picking years from a sequence of years

```{r}
# want to randomly start 20  fires in a fire model, locations numbered from 1 to 100
firestarts = runif(20, min=0, max=100)
ggplot(data.frame(firestarts=firestarts), aes(firestarts))+geom_histogram()


# want to mimic a population of 20 otters with a mean weight of 10 kg and a standard deviation of 2 kg
otterweight = rnorm(20, mean=10, sd=2)
ggplot(data.frame(otterweight), aes(otterweight))+geom_histogram()

# notice how the more you sample the more it looks normal
otterweight = rnorm(2000, mean=10, sd=2)
ggplot(data.frame(otterweight), aes(otterweight))+geom_histogram()


# sample allows you to pick from a vector of values, lets say we want to randomly pick years from a sequence of years from 2000 to 2025
# two options - we allow a year to be picked more than once
pick = sample(seq(2000,2025), size=20, replace=T)
pick
# no repeat years
pick = sample(seq(2000,2025), size=20, replace=F)
pick

```

## Now you try {.scrollable}

Write the R code to create test data where we are simulating how many bear-visitor encounters are likely, if we know that

- the number of encounters per day follows a Poisson distribution with a mean of 1 encounter per day

- there are 1000 visitors

We want to simulate how many encounters they have

**Poisson distribution** is a discrete distribution that describes the number of events that occur in a fixed interval of time or space, given a known average rate of occurrence (e.g lambda =1, in *rpois*)

Generate sample data and plot the distribution of encounters

Whats the maximum number of encounters any visitor had?

Add to quiz on Canvas

## <span style="color:orange">Data in R Packages {.scrollable}

- Many Packages in R have datasets associated with them

- To see this you can use the **data()** command

- To load a dataset from a package you can use **data(datasetname)**

- A useful thing to do is run **help(datasetname)** to learn about the dataset and its structure

- OR in R you can always use (**attributes**, **str**) to learn about the structure of the data

Lets try and then use *sample* to sample from one

```{r}

data()

# Lets look at New York Air Quality Measurement
# load into your R environment
data(airquality)
# learn about dataset
help(airquality)
# a good way to see structure of data
str(airquality)
attributes(airquality)

# does Ozone correlate with air temperature
ggplot(airquality, aes(x=Temp, y=Ozone))+geom_point()+ggtitle("Ozone vs Temperature")

# lets say we are testing a new product and we want a sample of 5 ozone measurement that conforms to the distribution in this dataset
ozone_sample = sample(airquality$Ozone, size=5, replace=F)
ozone_sample
# this works but we have some NA values - maybe subset
airquality_clean = na.omit(airquality)

# Issue 2 - we can't link that Ozone with other measurements in the dataset - we need to sample the row number instead of ozone
ozone_sample = sample(1:nrow(airquality_clean), size=5, replace=F)
ozone_sample
airquality_clean[ozone_sample,]
# plot our sample days

ggplot(airquality_clean, aes(x=Temp, y=Ozone))+geom_point()+
  geom_point(data=data.frame(Temp=airquality_clean$Temp[ozone_sample], Ozone=airquality_clean$Ozone[ozone_sample]), color="red", size=3)

```

## What we are learning {.scrollable}

- How to generate data (often needed for testing code)
- Some data cleaning practices
- Always plot your data after processing to make sure what you did makes sense

Practicing sampling from a dataset

On your own with the airquality data set

1)  find the median Wind speed
2)  Sample 10 Wind speed measurements
3)  compute their median

Submit both on Canvas quiz

## <span style="color:orange"> Data Types: Factors \span {.scrollabe}

something that has different **classes** or **groups** useful for doing calculations with categories

Here's an example:

First lets look at a standard numeric vector

```{r}
a = c(1.3, 1, 4, 1.3, 22)
# compute the mean
mean(a)
```

What if **a** is a factor

What do commands like **mean** do

```{r}
a = as.factor(a)
# compute the mean
mean(a)

#why? lets look
a




```

## Exploring factors {.scrollable}

We can use **summary** with factors to get frequencies in each category (or “level” )

```{r fishes}

# create vector of possible fish 
possible.fish = c("salmon","steelhead","shark","tuna","cod")

# we can use sample to simulate a random recording of catch by fisherman, lets say we pick 20 fish from the net

catch1 = base::sample(possible.fish, size=20, replace=T)
# because possible.fish was a factor catch1 will be a factor
catch1

summary(catch1)
# if we want summary to be more useful - make this a factor
catch1 = as.factor(catch1)


# to quickly get frequencies of different fish and to plot 
summary(catch1)
plot(catch1, col="blue")


# we can also use summary to explore and return information about the distribution
# mean frequency of a particular type of fish
mean(summary(catch1))

# maximum frequency
max(summary(catch1))

# which fish was most frequently caught
which.max(summary(catch1))

#to get just the name 
names(which.max(summary(catch1)))

```

## On your own {.scrollable}

In a quarto document

- create a new population of some creature (could be mythical)
- sample from the population
- use summary to explore the distribution of your sample

## But I don't want a factor

Sometimes you don't want factors and R thinks something should be How to change back? **as.numeric** makes sense ...but

```{r, echo=TRUE}

a = as.factor(c(1.3, 1, 4, 1.3, 22))
#sum(a)

# try to make a numeric version from the factor
b = as.numeric(a)
sum(b)
b

# better
b = as.character(a)
b = as.numeric(b)
b
sum(b)
```

## <span style="color:orange">Strings and how to work with them {.scrollable}

Strings are character data types that are used to represent text

```{r}
examplestring = "River Otters"
nchar(examplestring)
```

All programming languages have ways to deal with strings Some commonly used R functions

- **paste** for concatenating strings together
- **strsplit** for splitting strings into parts
- **sprintf** for creating formatted strings
- **gsub** for replacing parts of strings
- **tolower** and **toupper** for changing case
- **nchar** for counting the number of characters in a string

Most of these are easy to use - just look at help for the function and try it out

**sprintf** however takes some specialized knowledge

## Creating strings with sprintf. {.scrollable}

**sprintf** used something called *format string syntax* to create formatted strings

*format string syntax* is used in many places -for searching for and creating strings - AND - its used by many programming languages

- C / C++ → printf
- Python → old-style % formatting and str.format()
- Java / JavaScript → similar formatting ideas
- SQL (some dialects),
- Unix Shell (terminal window)

## format string syntax {.scrollable}

*sprintf* uses format 'code' to tell it how to create a string, based on the values you give it

Example

```{r}
sprintf("We found %d %s %3.1f miles from shore", 4,"Otters", 18.95632342)
```

- **%s** replace with a string
- **%d** replace with an integer value
- **%f** replace with a real value
- **%4.1f** replace with a real value with 4 digits, two after decimal
- **\\n** add a line return

## Example {.scrollable}

make a string with **sprintf** and add to plot title

```{r}
# use results for creating text
# sprintf creates a string %s mean use what ever is after the , to fill in a string
plottitle=sprintf("We like %s", names(which.max(summary(catch1))))

ggplot(data.frame(catch1=catch1), aes(catch1, fill=catch1))+geom_histogram(stat="count")+labs(title=plottitle)

# you can also add numbers to the string
plottitle=sprintf("We mostly caught %s \n max catch(%d)", names(which.max(summary(catch1))), max(summary(catch1)))
ggplot(data.frame(catch1=catch1), aes(catch1, fill=catch1))+geom_histogram(stat="count")+labs(title=plottitle)

```

## Another Example with Sprintf {.scrollable}

1.  Try making a plot with the airquality dataset
2.  add a title that includes

- the mean Ozone level in the dataset,
- a line return
- the mean Ozone level in the sample you took earlier

```{r, anothersprintfexample}

title = sprintf("Mean Ozone in dataset: %4.1f \n Mean Ozone in sample: %4.1f", mean(airquality$Ozone, na.rm=T), mean(airquality_clean$Ozone[ozone_sample], na.rm=T))
title

ggplot(airquality_clean, aes(x=Temp, y=Ozone))+geom_point()+
  geom_point(data=data.frame(Temp=airquality_clean$Temp[ozone_sample], Ozone=airquality_clean$Ozone[ozone_sample]), color="red", size=3) +ggtitle(title)
```

## <span style="color:orange"> Lists \span {.scrollable}

- Lists are the most “informal” data structures in R
- List are really useful for keeping track of and organizing groups of things that are not all the same
- A list could be a table where number of rows is different for each column
- A list can have numeric, character, factors all mixed together
- List are often used for returning more complex information from function (e.g. lm)

## Example of lists {.scrollable}

```{r introlist, echo=TRUE}

# make a list
sale = list(id=2, quality="high", contents=c("apple","cherry"), cost=c(4,5))
sale

#ways to access elements
sale$id
sale$what

# you can also access by list item number
# the [x] denotes the xth item in the list
sale[[3]]
sale[["contents"]]


# how do you get the second element in the vector that has the contents
# there are two ways


# add to a list
sale$location = "Farmers market"
sale
# or remove
sale$location = NULL
sale

# some tricky things
# correct accessing items in list
sale$cost
sale[[4]]

# works but
#sale[4]


sum(sale$cost)
sum(sale[[4]])

```

## <span style="color:orange"> What we've explored \span {.scrollable}

- common data types
- ways to generate data
- string format syntax
- lists
